Nge doc page

4 min read
16 hours ago

--

One folder of Markdown, in the same repo as the code: the spec the AI designs and tests against, the docs site I ship, and the RAG behind my in-app chatbot.
We have been saying it for years: documentation should live next to the code. Docs-as-code, same repo, reviewed in the same pull request. Good advice, and still not enough, because the docs lag. The code changes in the PR, the docs get a “TODO: update later,” and three sprints on, the two describe different products.
The AI era finally gave me a reason to close that gap, and a strange new incentive to keep it closed: the AI is now one of the readers, and one of the writers.
Here is how I work now. It started as a convenience and became the backbone of how I build.
The setup: product docs as plain Markdown, in the repo
My product documentation is plain Markdown, one page per feature, each with a small frontmatter block. It lives in the same repository as the code it describes. Nothing exotic: text files, versioned with the thing they document.
That frontmatter is not decoration. It is structured metadata that later feeds retrieval. Hold that thought.
The loop: the docs are the spec the AI builds from
When I ask an AI assistant to build a feature, it does not start from my prompt alone. It starts from that product documentation as the source of truth. It designs against it, writes tests against it, audits the change against it. And at the end of a delivery, it updates the docs to match what actually shipped.
Read that last part again, because it is the whole trick. The documentation is an input to the work and an output of it. Code and docs cannot drift, because the same pass that changes one changes the other. The spec, the implementation, and the description of the implementation converge by construction.
It also makes the AI better. A model handed a clear, current spec designs more coherently and audits more honestly than one guessing from a diff. The docs are the context. Keeping them sharp is not overhead, it is how I get reliable code.
The publish: same Markdown, automatic siteThe requirements were small
When I ship to production, those Markdown files are pulled into a documentation site automatically: navigation, search, the works. The published site is generated from the exact files the code was built against, so the docs in production always match the product in production. There is no separate “update the docs site” task, because there is no separate docs site. Depending on the project, that site can carry technical documentation next to the product docs, and sit behind a login.
This step is the reason I rebuilt nge-doc. My Angular docs library had existed for years, but this way of working needed something precise from it: point it at the Markdown folder, get a static site on deploy, and emit machine-readable versions of the same content, an llms.txt and an MCP server, so the docs the AI writes are docs an AI can also read back. I redesigned it around exactly that. You can see the result on its own docs.
The third job: the frontmatter becomes a chatbot
Remember the frontmatter. Because every page carries clean metadata and clean Markdown, the same corpus is already shaped for retrieval. If I want a documentation chatbot inside the app, the RAG is sitting right there: labelled, well-chunked pages, no scraping, no separate pipeline. The docs that specced the feature and shipped the site also answer questions about it.
One folder. Three jobs. The spec the AI builds from, the site users read, and the knowledge base the assistant retrieves from. The same files, always in sync, because they are literally the same files.
Why this holds together
Take any one piece away and it is a nice habit. Together they are a system:

• Docs in the repo, reviewed and versioned with the code.

• Docs as the AI’s source of truth, so design, tests, and audits share one definition of the product, and the docs get updated as part of shipping.

• Docs auto-published, so what users see always matches production.

• Docs structured for retrieval, so an in-app assistant answers from the truth, not from guesses.
The result is the thing every team claims to want and few actually have: a single source of truth that is mechanically, always up to date. Plus code that is more reliable, because the AI that wrote it worked from a spec instead of vibes.
Markdown is what makes it possible. It is the one format a human reads, a diff tracks, a static site renders, and a language model ingests without ceremony. Choose a heavier format and one of those readers falls out.
The practice, not the platform
None of this needs a big platform: Markdown files, a frontmatter convention, an AI assistant pointed at them, and a build step that publishes the folder on deploy. nge-doc is just the piece I happened to need for the Angular world. The tool is the small part. The way of working is the point.
Your documentation is about to have more readers than it ever had, and some of them write your code now. Treat it like the source of truth it already is.
